question.text: Mrs. Gupta, a retired teacher, wants to recommend a mutual fund scheme to a conservative client. The scheme's benchmark is Nifty 50, and the scheme's returns for the last 3 years were: Year 1: 12% (Nifty: 10%), Year 2: 8% (Nifty: 9%), Year 3: 15% (Nifty: 14%). Can she sell this scheme as a new cadre distributor?

answer.text: Yes, because the overall 3-year return is higher than Nifty
No, because the scheme underperformed its benchmark in Year 2
Yes, because 2 out of 3 years had better returns
No, because only index funds can be sold

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The requirement is that the scheme must have returns equal to or better than the benchmark for EACH of the last 3 years — not on average. In Year 2, the scheme returned 8% while Nifty returned 9%, so the scheme underperformed. This means it does NOT meet the eligibility criteria. **The Trap:** Looking at the overall or average return might make the scheme appear eligible. But the rule requires outperformance in EACH year individually — not just on average. **Cross-Exam Context:** This is also tested in Series V-A (MF Distributors) under scheme eligibility for distribution. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always check year-by-year benchmark comparison before recommending a scheme. A scheme that underperforms even in one of the three years is not eligible for you to sell.
